page: Vultr vbm-6c-128gb is a bare metal server belonging to the Bare Metal SSD family. It features a dedicated Intel Xeon 2386G processor built on the x86_64 architecture, operating at a base clock speed of 3.5 GHz. The system provides 12 physical cores and 12 virtual CPUs with a single thread per core configuration. This dedicated hardware allocation ensures that compute resources are not shared with other tenants, eliminating virtualization overhead and noisy-neighbor performance degradation.

The server is equipped with 128.0 GB of system memory, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 10.67 GB per vCPU. Storage is provided via a bundled 1920 GB local solid-state drive (SSD), which delivers local storage capacity directly attached to the bare metal instance. Additionally, the configuration includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. There are no integrated graphics processing units (GPUs) on this model.

Given the combination of dedicated Intel Xeon processing, a high memory-to-core ratio, and local SSD storage, this bare metal server is designed for workloads requiring direct hardware access and high memory density. It is suitable for hosting relational databases, memory-intensive caching layers, enterprise applications, and dedicated web serving environments. The bare metal architecture provides qualitative cost efficiency by offering raw, non-virtualized resource density and predictable performance without hypervisor overhead.
description: Vultr vbm-6c-128gb is a dedicated bare metal server from the Bare Metal SSD family, featuring an Intel Xeon 2386G processor with 12 physical cores and 12 vCPUs running at 3.5 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. It is configured with 128.0 GB of RAM, providing a high memory-to-core ratio of 10.67 GB per vCPU, alongside a bundled 1920 GB local SSD for storage. Lacking virtualization overhead, this server delivers dedicated, non-shared compute resources and includes one public IPv4 address. The hardware profile is optimized for memory-intensive and storage-reliant workloads, making it highly suitable for database hosting, enterprise applications, and caching systems. By eliminating hypervisor layers, it offers predictable performance and efficient resource utilization for demanding deployments.
